          Programming note: Money Stuff will be off tomorrow and Monday, ba=
ck on Tuesday.WhaleFarm RugPullHere=E2=80=99s a thing you can do. You start=
 a company and sell some stock to the public. You say =E2=80=9Cwe are a goo=
d company and we plan to do good things, give us money to do them.=E2=80=9D=
 You sell, say, 10% of the company to the public at a low price. You keep t=
he other 90% for yourself, as the founder of the company. Then you start pu=
tting out press releases saying =E2=80=9Cwe did a bunch of good things!=E2=
=80=9D These press releases are not true. Also you buy a bit of the publicl=
y traded stock for yourself, to create volume and move the price up. Invest=
ors read the press releases and see the buying activity, and they think the=
 stock is good. So they=C2=A0buy it. The stock =E2=80=94=C2=A0the 10% that =
is publicly traded =E2=80=94 goes up. The price is now high. Then what you =
do is, you sell your stock =E2=80=94=C2=A0the 90% that you kept =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0at the new high price. Then you close up the company and move on to y=
our next scam.This is a thing that people very much do. It is generally cal=
led a =E2=80=9Cpump and dump.=E2=80=9D There are some obstacles to doing it=
. A lot of them are legal and regulatory restrictions. Generally you can=E2=
=80=99t start a company and sell stock to the public without registering it=
 with the Securities and Exchange Commission, hiring an auditor, etc. Also =
if you keep 90% of the stock for yourself, you can=E2=80=99t sell=C2=A0that=
=C2=A0stock without a prospectus, which means disclosing that you (the foun=
der) are selling. Also it is securities fraud to put out press releases ful=
l of lies. Also it is market manipulation to buy stock for the purpose of p=
ushing up the price and creating misleading volume. People very much do pum=
p and dumps, but they also very much get in trouble for them. (Sometimes.)B=
ut there is another limitation on pump and dumps, which is that the market =
for these companies is usually pretty small. When you decide to dump =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0to sell your stock =E2=80=94=C2=A0who will buy it? In the first in=
stance the answer is =E2=80=9Cmarket makers,=E2=80=9D generally high-freque=
ncy electronic trading firms that stand ready to buy or sell stock on the e=
xchange. But they will generally not provide all that much liquidity in som=
e weird small company, and they will move their prices quickly in the face =
of sustained selling. The only way to really get out is to have fundamental=
 buyers: The people you pumped the stock to need to keep buying even as you=
 are selling. You need to deceive people not just on the way up =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0the pump =E2=80=94=C2=A0but also on the way down =E2=80=94=C2=A0the d=
ump. The dump takes time, and during that time you need buyers so you can s=
ell your stock.Obviously this is not impossible =E2=80=94=C2=A0again, peopl=
e do this a lot =E2=80=94=C2=A0but it requires some finesse. It is unpredic=
table; you can do a bunch of pumping and then turn around and dump and find=
 that there=E2=80=99s no one there to actually buy. The fact that the price=
 went up during the pump doesn=E2=80=99t=C2=A0prove=C2=A0that it will stay =
up =E2=80=94=C2=A0or decline linearly =E2=80=94=C2=A0during the dump; deman=
d might collapse unpredictably all at once.Decentralized finance is a crypt=
o-driven revolution in finance in which assets =E2=80=94=C2=A0mainly crypto=
 tokens =E2=80=94=C2=A0trade on decentralized exchanges using smart contrac=
ts=C2=A0without any trusted intermediary. One aspect of DeFi is automated m=
arket making. Instead of some electronic trading firm making markets with i=
ts own proprietary capital, there will be some smart contract that makes ma=
rkets on an algorithmic basis.So there will be some token, call it PumpCoin=
, and people will want to buy or sell it for some more commonly used token,=
 say Ethereum. Someone will create a smart contract that will buy PumpCoin =
(paying Ethereum)=C2=A0from anyone who wants to sell it, or sell PumpCoin (=
for Ethereum) to anyone who wants to buy it.=C2=A0Where does the automated =
market maker get the money to do this? Typically, it will have a liquidity =
pool of tokens that are deposited into the smart contract. Anyone who wants=
 to can deposit tokens into the smart contract. Ordinarily you deposit some=
 of=C2=A0both tokens in the pair =E2=80=94=C2=A0both PumpCoin and Ethereum =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0to participate in the automated market maker. And then anyon=
e who wants to buy or sell PumpCoins can trade with the automated market ma=
ker, and=C2=A0the automated market maker charges a fee to trade. And if you=
 have put money into the liquidity pool, you get a share of the fees.=C2=A0=
How does the automated market maker decide on the exchange rate between Pum=
pCoin and Ethereum? Well, there are some simple models of market making. Ba=
sically you start at some price =E2=80=94=C2=A0some exchange rate that refl=
ects initial market conditions =E2=80=94=C2=A0and then see what people do. =
If everyone comes to you to buy PumpCoin, you are charging too little, so y=
ou should raise the price of PumpCoin; if everyone comes to sell, you are p=
aying too much, so you should lower the price. Every time you buy you shoul=
d lower the price a little; every time you sell you should raise the price =
a little; if you do this then your book should stay roughly in balance. DeF=
i automated market makers often=C2=A0implement this idea using a =E2=80=9Cc=
onstant product formula,=E2=80=9D a very simple arithmetic rule=C2=A0calcul=
ating the exchange ratio from the number of each token in the liquidity poo=
l.Here=E2=80=99s a thing you can do. You start a new token and list it on a=
 DeFi platform. You say =E2=80=9Cwe are a good token and we plan to do good=
 things.=E2=80=9D Some tokens trade publicly, and you keep a lot for yourse=
lf as the developer of the token. Then you start putting out press releases=
 =E2=80=94=C2=A0I mean, Telegram posts or whatever =E2=80=94 saying =E2=80=
=9Clook how good this token is!=E2=80=9D People start putting money into th=
e liquidity pool of an automated market maker that exchanges your token (Pu=
mpCoin)=C2=A0for some more widely used token (Ethereum). You buy a bit of P=
umpCoin=C2=A0for yourself, to create volume and move the price up. DeFi inv=
estors=C2=A0read your hype=C2=A0and see the buying activity, and they think=
 the token is good. (The technical DeFi term appears to be =E2=80=9CFOMO,=
=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9Cfear of missing out,=E2=80=9D which is=C2=A0slightly dif=
ferent=C2=A0from actually thinking it is good =E2=80=A6=C2=A0for =E2=80=A6=
=C2=A0something.)=C2=A0So they=C2=A0buy it; more important, though, they ke=
ep depositing money into the liquidity pool.=C2=A0The price is now high, an=
d there is a lot of money in the liquidity pool. Then what you do is, you s=
ell your tokens =E2=80=94=C2=A0the ones=C2=A0that you kept =E2=80=94 for al=
l the Ethereum in the liquidity pool. There is an automated market maker, a=
 smart contract that has a bunch of Ethereum and will give it to you, for P=
umpCoins, on a purely formulaic basis. You take advantage of this by sellin=
g PumpCoins to the smart contract until it has no Ethereum left. Then you c=
lose up the project and move on to your next scam.This is a thing that peop=
le very much do. It is called a =E2=80=9Crug pull.=E2=80=9D It has =E2=80=
=A6=C2=A0certain =E2=80=A6=C2=A0advantages over the classic stock pump and =
dump. One is that legally crypto, and especially DeFi, is totally the Wild =
West, and there is no norm of, like, registering new tokens with the SEC an=
d getting them audited. I am not going to say that rug pulls are=C2=A0legal=
 =E2=80=94=C2=A0they seem=C2=A0=E2=80=A6=C2=A0not=C2=A0=E2=80=A6=C2=A0legal=
? =E2=80=94=C2=A0only that the law does not seem to be a huge practical lim=
itation. You can=E2=80=99t list stock on a stock exchange without registeri=
ng it with the SEC; you can list a token on a decentralized exchange withou=
t any approval from anyone.The other advantage is that the market maker is =
automated and has its liquidity pool all ready to go, which means that when=
 you decide to do the dump =E2=80=94=C2=A0the rug pull =E2=80=94=C2=A0you c=
an sell all your tokens at once into predictable demand. On the stock excha=
nge, if you want to sell some small stock, there might be quotes for a few =
hundred shares. You can sell a few hundred shares, but then you have to wai=
t to see if market makers refresh their quotes or if fundamental buyers com=
e in to buy more. The market makers =E2=80=94=C2=A0the electronic trading f=
irms =E2=80=94=C2=A0put only a tiny fraction of their capital on the line q=
uoting any particular stock, and if the stock moves in a weird way they may=
 not put up more. In DeFi, the entire capital of every automated market mak=
er is continually available. If you want to crush the price and take all th=
e money out at once, sure, go ahead. Anyway:On Monday, something called Wha=
leFarm was trading above $200 on crypto exchanges. By Wednesday, it was wor=
th close to zero.It=E2=80=99s the latest easy-come, easy-go DeFi project to=
 raise eyebrows, after its crash likely wiped out millions in value in a ma=
tter of hours, prompting Twitter lamentations on its obliteration and warni=
ngs from market pros on avoiding too-good-to-be true investments.=E2=80=9CT=
he whole thing is just fake -- people get fake yields, they get fake balanc=
es and then eventually the founders just take everything. A competitor plat=
form is offering 10%, so I say I can get you 20%. You send me your money an=
d then I run,=E2=80=9D said Stephane Ouellette, chief executive and co-foun=
der of FRNT Financial. =E2=80=9CAll the platforms are perpetuating this stu=
ff because it trades actively, but there is just so much junk and this is o=
nly going to continue to get worse.=E2=80=9DWhaleFarm was likely what=E2=80=
=99s known as a rug pull, a malign move that=E2=80=99s becoming more preval=
ent in the DeFi space whereby a developer abandons a project and absconds w=
ith the funds. In other words, the holder of the cryptocurrency in question=
 dumps their stake all at once, leading to a catastrophic price drop that w=
ipes out other investors.Here=E2=80=99s how it might play out, according to=
 Wilfred Daye, chief executive officer of Enigma Securities: A token develo=
per copies an existing smart contract code off a public venue and then issu=
es a platform token. The developer then markets it, lists it on a decentral=
ized exchange, and attracts higher-value coins like wrapped Bitcoin or Ethe=
r in what=E2=80=99s known as a liquidity pool. The developer then sells or =
redeems their platform token, depleting the liquidity pool of the project. =
They can then make off with the proceeds.=E2=80=9CObviously, investors need=
 to be vigilant about such DeFi products. There are always tell-tale signs,=
=E2=80=9D including promises of sky-high returns, copied codebases, and ano=
nymous teams, said Daye. =E2=80=A6Aaron Brown, a crypto investor who writes=
 for Bloomberg Opinion, said that not all the facts are in about WhaleFarm =
-- the team could, after all, reveal itself tomorrow and pay back all the m=
oney. But =E2=80=9Cwhile this is a sad story for the victims, it=E2=80=99s =
not a big deal. $2 million frauds are everyday occurrences,=E2=80=9D he sai=
d. =E2=80=9CIf the crooks aren=E2=80=99t doing them in crypto, they=E2=80=
=99re selling fake stock, or worthless gemstones, or phantom gold, or real =
estate they don=E2=80=99t own.=E2=80=9DHe=E2=80=99s not wrong. Still, my po=
int in going through the mechanics like that is that DeFi seems to offer so=
me real innovation in making these sorts of scams more efficient, predictab=
le and reliable. Old-school finance relied on trust and=C2=A0messy human in=
tervention; DeFi automates a lot of those processes, which can make scammin=
g easier.RobinhoodA thing that Robinhood Financial LLC really did was send =
a 20-year-old customer a mistaken account statement saying that he owed Rob=
inhood $750,000 for a losing trade, causing him to panic and email customer=
 service repeatedly to ask if it was true; when customer service ignored hi=
m, he committed suicide. =E2=80=9CThe day after Alex took his own life, Rob=
inhood sent an automated email suggesting the trade had been resolved and h=
e didn=E2=80=99t owe any money.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0This is old news =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0it happened a year ago; the quote above is from a CBS article from this =
February =E2=80=94=C2=A0and we haven=E2=80=99t really talked about it aroun=
d here, because it is very sad and because I do not have much to say about =
it. It does not fit into any interesting category of financial misconduct. =
It=E2=80=99s just bad customer service about something that is incredibly i=
mportant to your customers, literally a matter of life and death. But the s=
olution is not particularly complicated; it=E2=80=99s, like, double-check y=
our account statements and answer the phone.And the thing is, there are ple=
nty of interesting things to say about Robinhood, plenty of arcane and some=
times fanciful theories of Why Robinhood Is Bad. We talk from time to time =
about  Robinhood=E2=80=99s payment-for-order-flow relationships with high-f=
requency market makers; people love to get angry about those relationships,=
 often for bad reasons, but in fact last year  Robinhood got in trouble wit=
h the Securities and Exchange Commission for lying about them. At the heigh=
t of the GameStop drama, we talked about  Robinhood=E2=80=99s trouble meeti=
ng  clearing requirements, which led it to restrict trading in some meme st=
ocks for a little while. Some people attributed this=C2=A0to a nefarious co=
nspiracy theory involving hjgh-frequency traders and hedge funds, while oth=
ers got very emotional about T+2 settlement for like a week. In 2018, Robin=
hood briefly advertised=C2=A0fake bank accounts and  got in hilarious troub=
le.=C2=A0Robinhood just spends a lot of time living at the very edge of wha=
t is financially possible and legally permissible, and sometimes it gets in=
 weird trouble for it; this makes it interesting.But, more prosaically, the=
re is also a certain underbrush=C2=A0of =E2=80=A6 miscellaneous=C2=A0inepti=
tude? Robinhood signed up a lot of inexperienced customers and encouraged=
=C2=A0them to trade complicated options strategies; then it turned out that=
=C2=A0Robinhood didn=E2=80=99t understand those strategies itself=C2=A0and =
sent them incorrect account statements, with occasionally tragic results. O=
r, before Robinhood briefly shut down trading in meme stocks in February fo=
r complicated clearing reasons, it had a reputation for shutting down tradi=
ng in=C2=A0all=C2=A0stocks from time to time,  occasionally for an entire t=
rading day, particularly in volatile markets,=C2=A0just because its compute=
rs sometimes stopped working. If you are trying to buy or sell stocks in a =
volatile market, to avoid horrendous losses or to seize a once-in-a-lifetim=
e opportunity, it is very bad for your broker=E2=80=99s computers to break.=
=E2=80=9CTry not to have your website break=E2=80=9D is not a particularly =
interesting area of financial regulation, but, still, if you are a broker-d=
ealer you really should try not to have your website break. And you should =
not send people inaccurate account statements, not so much because that is =
likely to cause suicides but because keeping accurate books and records and=
 communicating=C2=A0them accurately to your clients are pretty basic functi=
ons of a securities broker. Robinhood has done a certain amount of very bas=
ic bad stuff that it seems like someone should fix, but that somehow seems =
to fall almost beneath financial regulation.=C2=A0But not quite; yesterday =
the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority fined Robinhood $57 million, an=
d ordered it to pay customers back $12.6 million plus interest, for an asso=
rtment of these sorts of things.=C2=A0=E2=80=9CThe fine imposed in this mat=
ter, the highest ever levied by FINRA, reflects the scope and seriousness o=
f Robinhood=E2=80=99s violations, including FINRA=E2=80=99s finding that Ro=
binhood communicated false and misleading information to millions of its cu=
stomers,=E2=80=9D says Finra.First, FINRA found in its investigation that, =
despite Robinhood=E2=80=99s self-described mission to =E2=80=9Cde-mystify f=
inance for all,=E2=80=9D during certain periods since September 2016, the f=
irm has negligently communicated false and misleading information to its cu=
stomers. =E2=80=A6 For instance, one Robinhood customer who had turned marg=
in =E2=80=9Coff,=E2=80=9D tragically took his own life in June 2020. In a n=
ote found after his death, he expressed confusion as to how he could have u=
sed margin to purchase securities because, he believed, he had not =E2=80=
=9Cturned on=E2=80=9D margin in his account. As noted in the settlement, Ro=
binhood also displayed to this individual (and certain other customers) ina=
ccurate negative cash balances. =E2=80=A6=C2=A0Second, FINRA found that sin=
ce Robinhood began offering options trading to customers in December 2017, =
the firm has failed to exercise due diligence before approving customers to=
 place options trades. The firm relied on algorithms=E2=80=94known at Robin=
hood as =E2=80=9Coption account approval bots=E2=80=9D=E2=80=94to approve c=
ustomers for options trading, with only limited oversight by firm principal=
s. Those bots often approved customers to trade options based on inconsiste=
nt or illogical information. As a result, Robinhood approved thousands of c=
ustomers for options trading who either did not satisfy the firm=E2=80=99s =
eligibility criteria or whose accounts contained red flags indicating that =
options trading may not have been appropriate for them.Third, FINRA found t=
hat, from January 2018 to February 2021, Robinhood failed to reasonably sup=
ervise the technology that it relied upon to provide core broker-dealer ser=
vices, such as accepting and executing customer orders. Between 2018 and la=
te 2020, Robinhood experienced a series of outages and critical systems fai=
lures. The most serious outage occurred on March 2 and 3, 2020, when Robinh=
ood=E2=80=99s website and mobile applications shut down, preventing Robinho=
od=E2=80=99s customers from accessing their accounts during a time of histo=
ric market volatility.Robinhood is planning an initial public offering, and=
 I suppose you have to clean all this stuff up before going public. It=E2=
=80=99s all better now:In a written statement outlining changes to its busi=
ness that Robinhood supplied to Finra, the brokerage firm said it had fixed=
 problems associated with displaying inaccurate balances and other data to =
customers. It also enhanced its oversight of customers=E2=80=99 use of opti=
ons, including through conducting monthly reviews to ensure clients meet el=
igibility rules, Robinhood said. =E2=80=A6The firm has taken steps to addre=
ss the causes of the outages and to reduce the risk of future ones, Robinho=
od wrote in the statement to Finra. It also now offers new customer-service=
 options, including the ability for customers to ask questions over the pho=
ne about options, according to the statement.Ideally you=E2=80=99d fix thes=
e things before rolling out your brokerage product to millions of people bu=
t here we are.

General housekeepingSorry, this is a little specialized, but I used to be a=
 big-firm lawyer, and a lot of my readers are big-firm lawyers, and this is=
 just a beautifully=C2=A0cutting thing for a big-firm lawyer to say about a=
nother big firm:A third lawyer, who has worked on Apollo transactions at a =
separate law firm, said Paul Weiss lawyers sometimes helped with "general h=
ousekeeping" on deals led by other firms. They would gather the appropriate=
 documentation from Apollo entities to consummate a transaction and offer t=
heir expertise from their institutional knowledge of Apollo as needed, rath=
er than being involved in strategic discussions between parties, this lawye=
r added."When we have a big deal we are leading, there is no doubt Paul Wei=
ss has involvement because they are so integrated within the Apollo organiz=
ation," this person said. "I frankly welcome their involvement."It=E2=80=99=
s from Casey Sullivan=E2=80=99s=C2=A0profile of the relationship between Pa=
ul, Weiss, Riftkind, Wharton & Garrison LLP, the big law firm, and Apollo G=
lobal Management Inc., the big private-equity firm. Apollo does a lot of ac=
quisitions, so it is a lucrative and=C2=A0potentially=C2=A0prestigious clie=
nt for the corporate department of a law firm. If you have Apollo as a clie=
nt and you advise Apollo on a lot of big deals, that is good. On the other =
hand if you have Apollo as a client and somebody=C2=A0else=C2=A0advises Apo=
llo on a lot of big deals, but they call you up to =E2=80=9Cgather the appr=
opriate documentation=E2=80=9D while they do the strategic discussions, tha=
t is =E2=80=A6=C2=A0less good. =E2=80=9CI frankly welcome their involvement=
=E2=80=9D is not=C2=A0exactly=C2=A0a compliment, coming from your competito=
r. You kind of want your competitors to say =E2=80=9Coh no, Paul Weiss is h=
ere, they=E2=80=99re gonna take over the deal, I hate them so much,=E2=80=
=9D not =E2=80=9Coh great, Paul Weiss is here, they=E2=80=99re gonna gather=
 the documents.=E2=80=9DNo no no, of course=C2=A0Paul Weiss is very prestig=
ious and has big-time mergers-and-acquisitions lawyers and advises Apollo o=
n tons of deals, in addition to doing the housekeeping on some other deals.=
 Still Sullivan=E2=80=99s article contains lots of griping from former Paul=
 Weiss associates about the housekeeping:One former associate expressed fru=
stration with the monotony of the work, saying that some of it amounted to =
little more than clerical duties, such as preparing the signature pages on =
deals, while another said that they felt spread thin across more deals than=
 they could reasonably handle.=C2=A0And:Some lawyers who worked in the Apol=
lo practice group said a number of colleagues found themselves stuck becaus=
e of their assignments to service routine work for Apollo. And they said th=
e group's singular focus meant colleagues weren't developing other client c=
ontacts that could become a book of business =E2=80=94 a key asset to an at=
torney's career."You often aren't on partner track if you're in that group,=
 and you work terrible hours," the second recruiter said.This is sort of th=
e trade-off with private-equity firms as clients, for an investment banker =
or a lawyer. The advantage is that they do a lot of deals, so you keep busy=
 and make a lot of money. The disadvantage is that they ruthlessly outsourc=
e=C2=A0bad work to their bankers and outside counsel, so you keep busy doin=
g boring things and you=E2=80=99re on call at all hours.Antitrust AIWe  tal=
ked yesterday about a rumored antitrust investigation into the banks that o=
wned stock on behalf of Archegos Capital Management. Archegos borrowed a bu=
nch of money from a handful of banks to buy=C2=A0a bunch of stock, then ski=
pped town, leaving the banks holding a lot of stock. They sold all the stoc=
k in fire sales, the prices crashed, there was a certain amount of panic an=
d concern. But before doing that, they briefly got together to say: What if=
 we=C2=A0didn=E2=80=99t=C2=A0do that? If we all sell our stock, the stocks =
will crash, which is bad; for market stability and confidence and our own f=
inancial health, we should wait and sell off gradually. That did not work =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0some banks sold, and then so did everyone else =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0but it seemed worth a try. Except that it is also maybe an illegal antit=
rust conspiracy?=C2=A0This is a form of a general=C2=A0problem in antitrust=
. Sometimes competitors in an industry will want to get together to agree n=
ot to do something that would be bad for the industry, or for the world. Th=
is is tricky to do, as an antitrust matter, because it is generally illegal=
 for a bunch of competitors to get together to agree to keep supply down or=
 prices up, or to agree not to give customers certain features. Often agree=
ing to hold off on doing something bad on the world can=C2=A0look like=C2=
=A0an agreement to restrict supply.So for instance if you work at an artifi=
cial-intelligence company, and a customer comes to you and says =E2=80=9Che=
y would you build me an evil AI that will enslave humankind, I=E2=80=99ll p=
ay you a lot of money,=E2=80=9D you might say no. But you might worry that =
your competitors would say yes, and then they=E2=80=99d get a lot of money =
and you wouldn=E2=80=99t. So you might want to get together with all your c=
ompetitors and agree that, even if a customer asks very nicely and offers a=
 lot of money, none of you will ever build an evil AI that will enslave hum=
ankind. But is that an agreement in restraint of trade? Are you colluding t=
o restrict the supply of AIs that customers want (evil enslavement AIs)?Her=
e is=C2=A0a fun paper from last July by Cullen O=E2=80=99Keefe of OpenAI ab=
out =E2=80=9CAntitrust-Compliant AI Industry Self-Regulation=E2=80=9D:Of in=
terest here is a hypothetical and potentially desirable horizontal agreemen=
t (=E2=80=9CAgreement=E2=80=9D) between AI engineers (or those working for =
labs aiming to produce AGI) not to produce unsafe A(G)I. =E2=80=A6=C2=A0The=
re is a colorable argument that an Agreement would correct a market failure=
 (namely, information asymmetry). AI engineers are much better-positioned t=
han their clients to know whether AI they produce is safe. Thus, an Agreeme=
nt would be analogous to the ethical canon at-issue in California Dental: i=
t would regulate informational asymmetries in the professionals=E2=80=99 ma=
rket.Note that the market failure is not the externalized risk to the publi=
c from unsafe AI. Under California Dental, the procompetitive benefits of t=
he Agreement must accrue to the consumers.If the above arguments are correc=
t, then California Dental dictates that the Agreement would be subject to r=
ule-of-reason review. The defendants would then have to show that its proco=
mpetitive effects outweigh its anticompetitive effects.That is, you can=E2=
=80=99t have an industry rule saying =E2=80=9Cno evil enslavement AIs=E2=80=
=9D just because you don=E2=80=99t want to enslave humanity; the rule has t=
o be justified on the grounds that you know better about what the customer =
wants (non-enslavement) than the customer does.One lesson you could take aw=
ay from this is that antitrust law is sort of=C2=A0generative=C2=A0of other=
 forms of government regulation. If everyone in the AI business thinks ther=
e should be a rule against evil AI, it is risky for them to just get togeth=
er and agree on it. But if some government agency regulates AI, then all th=
e people in the business can lobby that agency to make a rule about it.=C2=
=A0(Similarly, if everyone in the taxi industry thinks that there should be=
 safety regulations,=C2=A0or minimum-price regulations, it=E2=80=99s better=
 for them to lobby a taxi commission to make those rules than to agree on t=
hem themselves.) It is tricky for industries to self-regulate, because self=
-regulation might look like collusion, so they have to ask the government t=
o regulate instead.FlokiHere is a =E2=80=A6=C2=A0terrible? =E2=80=A6=C2=A0T=
witter thread about how finance works in 2021:=C2=A0=09Elon Musk tweets som=
ething.=09Someone launches a new token on a cryptocurrency platform like Un=
iswap.=09The token goes up for a while, as people read Musk=E2=80=99s tweet=
 and say =E2=80=9CElon Musk used a word, I must buy a cryptocurrency of tha=
t word.=E2=80=9DThe specific example is Floki, a meme coin whose name is wh=
at Elon Musk tweeted he would name a Shiba Inu. Or, apparently, there are s=
everal meme coins named Floki? Here's one that seems to have launched on Mo=
nday and now has a total value of=C2=A0$10 million? I hate it a lot.When El=
on Musk tweeted about Signal, the app, and the price of Signal Advance Inc.=
, a totally unrelated stock, went up a lot and stayed up for a while,  I wr=
ote:I suspect that the Elon Musk tweet didn=E2=80=99t really confuse anyone=
; it just provided a point to coordinate around. =E2=80=9CHahaha let=E2=80=
=99s trade this word that Elon Musk tweeted, that=E2=80=99ll be fun,=E2=80=
=9D is a plausible thought process. This is stock trading totally divorced =
from news and financial logic and corporate information; this is stock trad=
ing as a mix of trolling and gambling. It is the logical endpoint of the bo=
redom market hypothesis.But cryptocurrency and ERC20 tokens let people do t=
hat about=C2=A0every=C2=A0word that Elon Musk tweets. If Elon Musk tweets a=
 name for a dog, there will not necessarily be a stock with that name, but =
you can easily create an ERC20 token with that name, and then people can tr=
ade it. Not because they think that Musk has endorsed the token and plans t=
o accept it as payment for Teslas, but because they want to play the game o=
f =E2=80=9Ctrade the word that Elon Musk tweeted=E2=80=9D too. There is no =
content to this at all; it is a low-stakes gambling game that is fun becaus=
e it is also a joke about Elon Musk.Things happenAs Hertz  Exits Bankruptcy=
, Reddit Crowd Pockets a Big Score. TPG Is Evaluating a Public Listing. Fir=
ms Scrub  Dirty Bonds Off Books to Boost ESG Credentials. Point72 Seeks  Cr=
ypto Head While Soros Starts Bitcoin Trading. Trump Organization CFO  Surre=
nders to Authorities in New York. ECB to lift cap on eurozone banks=E2=80=
=99 dividends and share buybacks. U.K. Proposes Break From  MiFID Trading R=
ules to Boost Finance. High-Frequency Trader Hudson River to Execute Retail=
 Stock Trades. Amazon Seeks Recusal of FTC Chairwoman Lina Khan in Antitrus=
t Investigations of Company. Private equity breaks 40-year record with $500=
bn of deals. Internet=E2=80=99s Original Source Code Sold as NFT for $5.4 M=
illion. Man arrested after dipping sauce error leads to bomb threat. We got=
 flying cars.=C2=A0If you'd like to get=C2=A0Money=C2=A0Stuff=C2=A0in handy=
 email form, right in your inbox, please=C2=A0subscribe at this link. Or yo=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><strong>Programming note:</strong> <em>Money S=
tuff will be off tomorrow and Monday, back on Tuesday.</em></p><h2 style=3D=
"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial, Helvetica,=
 sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bottom-width: 1p=
x; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px 5px;">WhaleF=
arm RugPull</h2><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here=E2=80=99s a thing you can=
 do. You start a company and sell some stock to the public. You say =E2=80=
=9Cwe are a good company and we plan to do good things, give us money to do=
 them.=E2=80=9D You sell, say, 10% of the company to the public at a low pr=
ice. You keep the other 90% for yourself, as the founder of the company. Th=
en you start putting out press releases saying =E2=80=9Cwe did a bunch of g=
ood things!=E2=80=9D These press releases are not true. Also you buy a bit =
of the publicly traded stock for yourself, to create volume and move the pr=
ice up. Investors read the press releases and see the buying activity, and =
they think the stock is good. So they=C2=A0buy it. The stock =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0the 10% that is publicly traded =E2=80=94 goes up. The price is now high=
. Then what you do is, you sell your stock =E2=80=94=C2=A0the 90% that you =
kept =E2=80=94=C2=A0at the new high price. Then you close up the company an=
d move on to your next scam.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This is a thin=
g that people very much do. It is generally called a =E2=80=9Cpump and dump=
.=E2=80=9D There are some obstacles to doing it. A lot of them are legal an=
d regulatory restrictions. Generally you can=E2=80=99t start a company and =
sell stock to the public without registering it with the Securities and Exc=
hange Commission, hiring an auditor, etc. Also if you keep 90% of the stock=
 for yourself, you can=E2=80=99t sell=C2=A0<em>that=C2=A0</em>stock without=
 a prospectus, which means disclosing that you (the founder) are selling. A=
lso it is securities fraud to put out press releases full of lies. Also it =
is market manipulation to buy stock for the purpose of pushing up the price=
 and creating misleading volume. People very much do pump and dumps, but th=
ey also very much get in trouble for them. (Sometimes.)</p><p style=3D"marg=
in: 16px 0;">But there is another limitation on pump and dumps, which is th=
at the market for these companies is usually pretty small. When you decide =
to dump =E2=80=94=C2=A0to sell your stock =E2=80=94=C2=A0who will buy it? I=
n the first instance the answer is =E2=80=9Cmarket makers,=E2=80=9D general=
ly high-frequency electronic trading firms that stand ready to buy or sell =
stock on the exchange. But they will generally not provide all that much li=
quidity in some weird small company, and they will move their prices quickl=
y in the face of sustained selling. The only way to really get out is to ha=
ve fundamental buyers: The people you pumped the stock to need to keep buyi=
ng even as you are selling. You need to deceive people not just on the way =
up =E2=80=94=C2=A0the pump =E2=80=94=C2=A0but also on the way down =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0the dump. The dump takes time, and during that time you need buyer=
s so you can sell your stock.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Obviously thi=
s is not impossible =E2=80=94=C2=A0again, people do this a lot =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0but it requires some finesse. It is unpredictable; you can do a bunch of=
 pumping and then turn around and dump and find that there=E2=80=99s no one=
 there to actually buy. The fact that the price went up during the pump doe=
sn=E2=80=99t=C2=A0<em>prove=C2=A0</em>that it will stay up =E2=80=94=C2=A0o=
r decline linearly =E2=80=94=C2=A0during the dump; demand might collapse un=
predictably all at once.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Decentralized fina=
nce is a crypto-driven revolution in finance in which assets =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0mainly crypto tokens =E2=80=94=C2=A0trade on decentralized exchanges usi=
ng smart contracts=C2=A0without any trusted intermediary. One aspect of DeF=
i is <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/24330182.2708=
30/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZ2VtaW5pLmNvbS9jcnlwdG9wZWRpYS9hbW0td2hhdC1hcmUtYXV0b21h=
dGVkLW1hcmtldC1tYWtlcnMjc2VjdGlvbi1saXF1aWRpdHktcG9vbHMtYW5kLWxpcXVpZGl0eS1=
wcm92aWRlcnM/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB092444d3" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" item=
scope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !importa=
nt; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-s=
tyle: solid; background-color: #ccc;">automated market making</a>. Instead =
of some electronic trading firm making markets with its own proprietary cap=
ital, there will be some smart contract that makes markets on an algorithmi=
c basis.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">So there will be some token, call =
it PumpCoin, and people will want to buy or sell it for some more commonly =
used token, say Ethereum. Someone will create a smart contract that will bu=
y PumpCoin (paying Ethereum)=C2=A0from anyone who wants to sell it, or sell=
 PumpCoin (for Ethereum) to anyone who wants to buy it.=C2=A0</p><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Where does the automated market maker get the money to=
 do this? Typically, it will have a liquidity pool of tokens that are depos=
ited into the smart contract. Anyone who wants to can deposit tokens into t=
he smart contract. Ordinarily you deposit some of=C2=A0<em>both</em> tokens=
 in the pair =E2=80=94=C2=A0both PumpCoin and Ethereum =E2=80=94=C2=A0to pa=
rticipate in the automated market maker. And then anyone who wants to buy o=
r sell PumpCoins can trade with the automated market maker, and=C2=A0the au=
tomated market maker charges a fee to trade. And if you have put money into=
 the liquidity pool, you get a share of the fees.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"marg=
in: 16px 0;">How does the automated market maker decide on the exchange rat=
e between PumpCoin and Ethereum? Well, there are some simple models of mark=
et making. Basically you start at some price =E2=80=94=C2=A0some exchange r=
ate that reflects initial market conditions =E2=80=94=C2=A0and then see wha=
t people do. If everyone comes to you to buy PumpCoin, you are charging too=
 little, so you should raise the price of PumpCoin; if everyone comes to se=
ll, you are paying too much, so you should lower the price. Every time you =
buy you should lower the price a little; every time you sell you should rai=
se the price a little; if you do this then your book should stay roughly in=
 balance. DeFi automated market makers often=C2=A0implement this idea using=
 a =E2=80=9Cconstant product formula,=E2=80=9D a very <a href=3D"https://li=
nk.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/24330182.270830/aHR0cHM6Ly9yZXNlYXJjaC5=
wYXJhZGlnbS54eXovYW1tLXByaWNlLWltcGFjdA/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBeacaa584" =
itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000; tex=
t-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-colo=
r: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">simple ari=
thmetic rule</a>=C2=A0calculating the exchange ratio from the number of eac=
h token in the liquidity pool.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here=E2=80=
=99s a thing you can do. You start a new token and list it on a DeFi platfo=
rm. You say =E2=80=9Cwe are a good token and we plan to do good things.=E2=
=80=9D Some tokens trade publicly, and you keep a lot for yourself as the d=
eveloper of the token. Then you start putting out press releases =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0I mean, Telegram posts or whatever =E2=80=94 saying =E2=80=9Clook how=
 good this token is!=E2=80=9D People start putting money into the liquidity=
 pool of an automated market maker that exchanges your token (PumpCoin)=C2=
=A0for some more widely used token (Ethereum). You buy a bit of PumpCoin=C2=
=A0for yourself, to create volume and move the price up. DeFi investors=C2=
=A0read your hype=C2=A0and see the buying activity, and they think the toke=
n is good. (The technical DeFi term appears to be =E2=80=9CFOMO,=E2=80=9D =
=E2=80=9Cfear of missing out,=E2=80=9D which is=C2=A0<em>slightly different=
=C2=A0</em>from actually thinking it is good =E2=80=A6=C2=A0<em>for </em>=
=E2=80=A6=C2=A0something.)=C2=A0So they=C2=A0buy it; more important, though=
, they keep depositing money into the liquidity pool.=C2=A0The price is now=
 high, and there is a lot of money in the liquidity pool. Then what you do =
is, you sell your tokens =E2=80=94=C2=A0the ones=C2=A0that you kept =E2=80=
=94 for all the Ethereum in the liquidity pool. There is an automated marke=
t maker, a smart contract that has a bunch of Ethereum and will give it to =
you, for PumpCoins, on a purely formulaic basis. You take advantage of this=
 by selling PumpCoins to the smart contract until it has no Ethereum left. =
Then you close up the project and move on to your next scam.</p><p style=3D=
"margin: 16px 0;">This is a thing that people very much do. It is <a href=
=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/24330182.270830/aHR0cHM6L=
y9jb2lubWFya2V0Y2FwLmNvbS9hbGV4YW5kcmlhL2dsb3NzYXJ5L3J1Zy1wdWxs/607f07ceef4=
b8524a319a23cBe85c94b0" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" styl=
e=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width:=
 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-=
color: #ccc;">called</a> a =E2=80=9C<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergb=
usiness.com/click/24330182.270830/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuY3lseW54LmlvL2Jsb2cvdGhlL=
XJpc2Utb2YtY3J5cHRvY3VycmVuY3ktZXhpdC1zY2Ftcy1hbmQtZGVmaS1ydWctcHVsbHMv/607=
f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB70221c55" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemsco=
pe" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-botto=
m-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; bac=
kground-color: #ccc;">rug pull</a>.=E2=80=9D It has =E2=80=A6=C2=A0certain =
=E2=80=A6=C2=A0advantages over the classic stock pump and dump. One is that=
 legally crypto, and especially DeFi, is totally the Wild West, and there i=
s no norm of, like, registering new tokens with the SEC and getting them au=
dited. I am not going to say that rug pulls are=C2=A0<em>legal</em> =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0they seem=C2=A0=E2=80=A6=C2=A0not=C2=A0=E2=80=A6=C2=A0legal? =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0only that the law does not seem to be a huge practical limitati=
on. You can=E2=80=99t list stock on a stock exchange without registering it=
 with the SEC; you can list a token on a decentralized exchange without any=
 approval from anyone.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The other advantage =
is that the market maker is automated and has its liquidity pool all ready =
to go, which means that when you decide to do the dump =E2=80=94=C2=A0the r=
ug pull =E2=80=94=C2=A0you can sell all your tokens at once into predictabl=
e demand. On the stock exchange, if you want to sell some small stock, ther=
e might be quotes for a few hundred shares. You can sell a few hundred shar=
es, but then you have to wait to see if market makers refresh their quotes =
or if fundamental buyers come in to buy more. The market makers =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0the electronic trading firms =E2=80=94=C2=A0put only a tiny fraction =
of their capital on the line quoting any particular stock, and if the stock=
 moves in a weird way they may not put up more. In DeFi, the entire capital=
 of every automated market maker is continually available. If you want to c=
rush the price and take all the money out at once, sure, go ahead.</p><p st=
yle=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/=
click/24330182.270830/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzL=
zIwMjEtMDYtMzAvd2hhbGVmYXJtLWNyYXNoLWlzLWxhdGVzdC10b28tZ29vZC10by1iZS10cnVl=
LWRlZmktY29sbGFwc2U/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB65128d0f" itemprop=3D"StoryLin=
k" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !=
important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-b=
ottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> Anyway</a>:</p><blockquote><p=
 style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On Monday, something called WhaleFarm was tradin=
g above $200 on crypto exchanges. By Wednesday, it was worth close to zero.=
</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It=E2=80=99s the latest easy-come, easy-go=
 DeFi project to raise eyebrows, after its crash likely wiped out millions =
in value in a matter of hours, prompting Twitter lamentations on its oblite=
ration and warnings from market pros on avoiding too-good-to-be true invest=
ments.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CThe whole thing is just fak=
e -- people get fake yields, they get fake balances and then eventually the=
 founders just take everything. A competitor platform is offering 10%, so I=
 say I can get you 20%. You send me your money and then I run,=E2=80=9D sai=
d Stephane Ouellette, chief executive and co-founder of FRNT Financial. =E2=
=80=9CAll the platforms are perpetuating this stuff because it trades activ=
ely, but there is just so much junk and this is only going to continue to g=
et worse.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">WhaleFarm was likely wha=
t=E2=80=99s known as a rug pull, a malign move that=E2=80=99s becoming more=
 prevalent in the DeFi space whereby a developer abandons a project and abs=
conds with the funds. In other words, the holder of the cryptocurrency in q=
uestion dumps their stake all at once, leading to a catastrophic price drop=
 that wipes out other investors.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here=E2=80=
=99s how it might play out, according to Wilfred Daye, chief executive offi=
cer of Enigma Securities: A token developer copies an existing smart contra=
ct code off a public venue and then issues a platform token. The developer =
then markets it, lists it on a decentralized exchange, and attracts higher-=
value coins like wrapped Bitcoin or Ether in what=E2=80=99s known as a liqu=
idity pool. The developer then sells or redeems their platform token, deple=
ting the liquidity pool of the project. They can then make off with the pro=
ceeds.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CObviously, investors need t=
o be vigilant about such DeFi products. There are always tell-tale signs,=
=E2=80=9D including promises of sky-high returns, copied codebases, and ano=
nymous teams, said Daye. =E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Aaron Br=
own, a crypto investor who writes for Bloomberg Opinion, said that not all =
the facts are in about WhaleFarm -- the team could, after all, reveal itsel=
f tomorrow and pay back all the money. But =E2=80=9Cwhile this is a sad sto=
ry for the victims, it=E2=80=99s not a big deal. $2 million frauds are ever=
yday occurrences,=E2=80=9D he said. =E2=80=9CIf the crooks aren=E2=80=99t d=
oing them in crypto, they=E2=80=99re selling fake stock, or worthless gemst=
ones, or phantom gold, or real estate they don=E2=80=99t own.=E2=80=9D</p><=
/blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">He=E2=80=99s not wrong. Still, my =
point in going through the mechanics like that is that DeFi seems to offer =
some real innovation in making these sorts of scams more efficient, predict=
able and reliable. Old-school finance relied on trust and=C2=A0messy human =
intervention; DeFi automates a lot of those processes, which can make scamm=
ing easier.</p><h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Bla=
ck", Arial, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20=
px; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; pad=
ding: 16px 0px 5px;">Robinhood</h2><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A thing tha=
t Robinhood Financial LLC <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.co=
m/click/24330182.270830/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuY2JzbmV3cy5jb20vbmV3cy9hbGV4LWtlYXJ=
ucy1yb2Jpbmhvb2QtdHJhZGVyLXN1aWNpZGUtd3JvbmdmdWwtZGVhdGgtc3VpdC8/607f07ceef=
4b8524a319a23cB3da76f6a" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" sty=
le=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width=
: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background=
-color: #ccc;">really did</a> was send a 20-year-old customer a mistaken ac=
count statement saying that he owed Robinhood $750,000 for a losing trade, =
causing him to panic and email customer service repeatedly to ask if it was=
 true; when customer service ignored him, he committed suicide. =E2=80=9CTh=
e day after Alex took his own life, Robinhood sent an automated email sugge=
sting the trade had been resolved and he didn=E2=80=99t owe any money.=E2=
=80=9D=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This is old news =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0it happened a year ago; the quote above is from a CBS article from this =
February =E2=80=94=C2=A0and we haven=E2=80=99t really talked about it aroun=
d here, because it is very sad and because I do not have much to say about =
it. It does not fit into any interesting category of financial misconduct. =
It=E2=80=99s just bad customer service about something that is incredibly i=
mportant to your customers, literally a matter of life and death. But the s=
olution is not particularly complicated; it=E2=80=99s, like, double-check y=
our account statements and answer the phone.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;=
">And the thing is, there are plenty of interesting things to say about Rob=
inhood, plenty of arcane and sometimes fanciful theories of Why Robinhood I=
s Bad. We talk from time to time about <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombe=
rgbusiness.com/click/24330182.270830/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcG=
luaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDItMDUvcm9iaW5ob29kLWdhbWVzdG9wLXNhZ2EtcHJlc3N1c=
mVzLXBheW1lbnQtZm9yLW9yZGVyLWZsb3c/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB73ef0ef9" itemp=
rop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-dec=
oration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #0=
00000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> Robinhood=E2=
=80=99s payment-for-order-flow relationships</a> with high-frequency market=
 makers; people love to get angry about those relationships, often for bad =
reasons, but in fact last year <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusine=
ss.com/click/24330182.270830/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2=
FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDEtMDcvdGhlLWlwby1tYXJrZXQtd2FzLXRvby1nb29k/607f07ceef4b=
8524a319a23cB331953e9" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=
=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-c=
olor: #ccc;"> Robinhood got in trouble</a> with the Securities and Exchange=
 Commission for lying about them. At the height of the GameStop drama, we t=
alked about <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/243301=
82.270830/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtM=

a little specialized, but I used to be a big-firm lawyer, and a lot of my r=
eaders are big-firm lawyers, and this is just a beautifully=C2=A0cutting th=
ing for a big-firm lawyer to say about another big firm:</p><blockquote><p =
style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A third lawyer, who has worked on Apollo transact=
ions at a separate law firm, said Paul Weiss lawyers sometimes helped with =
"general housekeeping" on deals led by other firms. They would gather the a=
ppropriate documentation from Apollo entities to consummate a transaction a=
nd offer their expertise from their institutional knowledge of Apollo as ne=
eded, rather than being involved in strategic discussions between parties, =
this lawyer added.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">"When we have a big deal=
 we are leading, there is no doubt Paul Weiss has involvement because they =
are so integrated within the Apollo organization," this person said. "I fra=
nkly welcome their involvement."</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0=
;">It=E2=80=99s from Casey Sullivan=E2=80=99s=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://link.=

=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decora=

00; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">profile of the rel=
ationship</a> between Paul, Weiss, Riftkind, Wharton & Garrison LLP, th=
e big law firm, and Apollo Global Management Inc., the big private-equity f=
irm. Apollo does a lot of acquisitions, so it is a lucrative and=C2=A0<em>p=
otentially=C2=A0</em>prestigious client for the corporate department of a l=
aw firm. If you have Apollo as a client and you advise Apollo on a lot of b=
ig deals, that is good. On the other hand if you have Apollo as a client an=

t they call you up to =E2=80=9Cgather the appropriate documentation=E2=80=
=9D while they do the strategic discussions, that is =E2=80=A6=C2=A0less go=
od. =E2=80=9CI frankly welcome their involvement=E2=80=9D is not=C2=A0<em>e=
xactly=C2=A0</em>a compliment, coming from your competitor. You kind of wan=

re gonna take over the deal, I hate them so much,=E2=80=9D not =E2=80=9Coh =
great, Paul Weiss is here, they=E2=80=99re gonna gather the documents.=E2=
=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">No no no, of course=C2=A0Paul Weiss =

style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One former associate expressed frustration with t=

sonably handle.=C2=A0</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And:</p>=
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Some lawyers who worked in the Apo=
llo practice group said a number of colleagues found themselves stuck becau=

contacts that could become a book of business =E2=80=94 a key asset to an a=
ttorney's career.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">"You often aren't on part=
ner track if you're in that group, and you work terrible hours," the second=
 recruiter said.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This is sort =
of the trade-off with private-equity firms as clients, for an investment ba=
nker or a lawyer. The advantage is that they do a lot of deals, so you keep=
 busy and make a lot of money. The disadvantage is that they ruthlessly out=
source=C2=A0bad work to their bankers and outside counsel, so you keep busy=
 doing boring things and you=E2=80=99re on call at all hours.</p><h2 style=
=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial, Helveti=
ca, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bottom-width:=
 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px 5px;">Ant=
itrust AI</h2><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">We <a href=3D"https://link.mail.=
bloombergbusiness.com/click/24330182.270830/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmN=

tom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; b=
ackground-color: #ccc;"> talked yesterday</a> about a rumored antitrust inv=
estigation into the banks that owned stock on behalf of Archegos Capital Ma=
nagement. Archegos borrowed a bunch of money from a handful of banks to buy=
=C2=A0a bunch of stock, then skipped town, leaving the banks holding a lot =
of stock. They sold all the stock in fire sales, the prices crashed, there =
was a certain amount of panic and concern. But before doing that, they brie=
fly got together to say: What if we=C2=A0<em>didn=E2=80=99t=C2=A0</em>do th=
at? If we all sell our stock, the stocks will crash, which is bad; for mark=
et stability and confidence and our own financial health, we should wait an=
d sell off gradually. That did not work =E2=80=94=C2=A0some banks sold, and=
 then so did everyone else =E2=80=94=C2=A0but it seemed worth a try. Except=
 that it is also maybe an illegal antitrust conspiracy?=C2=A0</p><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This is a form of a general=C2=A0problem in antitrust.=
 Sometimes competitors in an industry will want to get together to agree no=
t to do something that would be bad for the industry, or for the world. Thi=
s is tricky to do, as an antitrust matter, because it is generally illegal =
for a bunch of competitors to get together to agree to keep supply down or =
prices up, or to agree not to give customers certain features. Often agreei=
ng to hold off on doing something bad on the world can=C2=A0<em>look like=
=C2=A0</em>an agreement to restrict supply.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"=
>So for instance if you work at an artificial-intelligence company, and a c=
ustomer comes to you and says =E2=80=9Chey would you build me an evil AI th=
at will enslave humankind, I=E2=80=99ll pay you a lot of money,=E2=80=9D yo=
u might say no. But you might worry that your competitors would say yes, an=

ight want to get together with all your competitors and agree that, even if=

er build an evil AI that will enslave humankind. But is that an agreement i=
n restraint of trade? Are you colluding to restrict the supply of AIs that =
customers want (evil enslavement AIs)?</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here=

270830/aHR0cHM6Ly9zdGF0aWMxLnNxdWFyZXNwYWNlLmNvbS9zdGF0aWMvNWJhNmUxODAxNmI2=

yle=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-widt=
h: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgroun=
d-color: #ccc;">a fun paper</a> from last July by Cullen O=E2=80=99Keefe of=
 OpenAI about =E2=80=9CAntitrust-Compliant AI Industry Self-Regulation=E2=
=80=9D:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Of interest here is a h=
ypothetical and potentially desirable horizontal agreement (=E2=80=9CAgreem=
ent=E2=80=9D) between AI engineers (or those working for labs aiming to pro=
duce AGI) not to produce unsafe A(G)I. =E2=80=A6=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margi=
n: 16px 0;">There is a colorable argument that an Agreement would correct a=
 market failure (namely, information asymmetry). AI engineers are much bett=
er-positioned than their clients to know whether AI they produce is safe. T=
hus, an Agreement would be analogous to the ethical canon at-issue in <em>C=
alifornia Dental</em>: it would regulate informational asymmetries in the p=
rofessionals=E2=80=99 market.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Note that the=
 market failure is <em>not</em> the externalized risk to the public from un=
safe AI. Under <em>California Dental</em>, the procompetitive benefits of t=
he Agreement must accrue to the <em>consumers</em>.</p><p style=3D"margin: =
16px 0;">If the above arguments are correct, then <em>California Dental </e=
m>dictates that the Agreement would be subject to rule-of-reason review. Th=
e defendants would then have to show that its procompetitive effects outwei=
gh its anticompetitive effects.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;=
">That is, you can=E2=80=99t have an industry rule saying =E2=80=9Cno evil =
enslavement AIs=E2=80=9D just because you don=E2=80=99t want to enslave hum=
anity; the rule has to be justified on the grounds that you know better abo=
ut what <em>the customer</em> wants (non-enslavement) than the customer doe=
s.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One lesson you could take away from this=
 is that antitrust law is sort of=C2=A0<em>generative=C2=A0</em>of other fo=
rms of government regulation. If everyone in the AI business thinks there s=
hould be a rule against evil AI, it is risky for them to just get together =
and agree on it. But if some government agency regulates AI, then all the p=
eople in the business can lobby that agency to make a rule about it.=C2=A0(=
Similarly, if everyone in the taxi industry thinks that there should be saf=
ety regulations,=C2=A0<em>or minimum-price regulations</em>, it=E2=80=99s b=
etter for them to lobby a taxi commission to make those rules than to agree=
